critique.summary: The article details the investigation and resolution of a complex bug causing periodic system freezes on Linux when using the Superluminal CPU profiler, linked to eBPF spinlock handling in the kernel. The root cause was a race condition in the resilient queued spinlock (rqspinlock) implementation, particularly involving non-maskable interrupts (NMIs) and recursive lock acquisition detection, which was fixed in kernel versions 6.17 to 6.19.
mainPoints: Superluminal CPU profiler caused periodic system freezes on Fedora 42 with Linux kernel 6.17.4-200 due to eBPF code interactions.
The freeze was traced to a spinlock deadlock in the Linux kernel's ring buffer implementation used by eBPF programs during context switch and sampling events.
The resilient queued spinlock (rqspinlock) was designed to detect and recover from deadlocks but had a bug in handling NMIs that caused missed recursive lock detection.
The bug was due to a race condition where the lock was acquired but not yet recorded in the held locks table before an NMI interrupted, causing the deadlock detection to miss the recursive lock.
A patch reordered lock acquisition and held lock table updates to fix this race, eliminating the 250ms freezes.
Further refinements addressed delayed deadlock detection checks and lock holder starvation caused by NMIs on other CPUs.
The fixes were incorporated into Linux kernel 6.19 and backported to 6.17 and 6.18, resolving the issue for affected users.
The article provides an in-depth explanation of spinlocks, queued spinlocks, and the challenges of locking in the kernel with eBPF and NMIs.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a detailed, technical, and accurate account of a real issue encountered with eBPF spinlocks in the Linux kernel, supported by kernel source code excerpts, explanations of kernel internals, and references to actual kernel patches and mailing list discussions. The timeline and kernel versions mentioned align with known Linux kernel development history. The explanations of spinlocks, rqspinlock, and NMI behavior are consistent with established kernel documentation and community knowledge. The involvement of recognized kernel maintainers and the patch references further support the article's credibility.
considerations: The article focuses on a specific kernel version range (6.17 to 6.19) and the issue may not be relevant to users on older or newer kernels.
The complexity of kernel locking and eBPF interactions means that similar issues could arise in other contexts not covered here.
The article does not discuss potential performance impacts of the fixes beyond eliminating freezes.
The debugging approach required specialized hardware (serial ports) which may not be accessible to all developers.
The article assumes reader familiarity with kernel development concepts; less experienced readers may find it challenging.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a detailed write-up about eBPF spinlock debugging in the Linux kernel, highlighting the complexity and challenges of static verification in kernel programming. Commenters appreciate the technical depth and clarity, while also suggesting potential improvements and noting the added complexity from container orchestration systems like Kubernetes.

comments.author: bubblerme
comments.text:
eBPF spinlock debugging is exactly the kind of kernel work that's simultaneously terrifying and fascinating. Spinlocks in eBPF programs are particularly tricky because you're operating in a context where you can't sleep, can't take mutexes, and the verifier needs to statically prove your lock usage is correct before the program even runs.

The verification challenge is the interesting part. The kernel verifier has to ensure that every path through the eBPF program properly acquires and releases locks, which is essentially solving a subset of the halting problem through conservative static analysis. False positives (rejecting valid programs) are acceptable; false negatives (allowing deadlocks) are not.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately describes the complexity of eBPF spinlock verification and the challenges of static analysis in kernel programming, providing a nuanced and technically sound perspective.

comments.author: sidkshatriya
comments.text:
Excellently explained writeup. Kudos on explaining the shockingly multiple kernel bugs in a (a) simple (b) interesting way.

TL;DR the main issue arises because the context switch and sampling event both need to be written to the `ringBuffer` eBPF map. sampling event lock needs to be taken in an NMI which is by definition non-maskable. This leads to lock contention and recursive locks etc as explained when context switch handler tries to do the same thing.

Why not have context switches write to ringBuffer1 and sampling events write to ringBuffer2 (i.e. use different ringBuffers). This way buggy kernels should work properly too !?
comments.analysis: This comment offers a detailed explanation of the specific kernel bug and suggests a plausible workaround, demonstrating a good understanding of the problem and potential solutions.

comments.author: legedemon
comments.text: Thanks for the great write-up with links to many more interesting articles and code! I have long stopped working on Linux kernel but deep dives like these are very exciting reading.
comments.analysis: While this comment is mostly appreciative and lacks technical detail, it reflects genuine engagement and interest in the topic, adding a positive tone to the discussion.

comments.author: stupefy
comments.text: It is a fantastic write up
comments.analysis: This brief comment expresses positive sentiment but does not contribute any substantive analysis or technical insight.
